coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of carbon dots in ten choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents: Effect of solvent molecular structure on carbon dots fluorescence and sensing properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Tabaraki, Reza
Nazari, Fereshteh
coredata.dc:description: Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have showed great interest in carbon dots synthesis. Surface and optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) can be influenced by solvent during the synthesis. In this study, microwave method was used for CDs synthesis in ten choline chloride based DESs. Glucose was used as a cheap carbon source. Surface functional groups, optical and fluorescence properties of ten CDs were compared. Quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs in choline chloride/urea, choline chloride/glycerol and choline chloride/thiourea DESs were 41.3, 38.7 and 35.5%, respectively. Doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms in the CDsDES1, CDsDES2 and CDsDES4 enhanced the photoluminescence performance. TEM, FT-IR, EDXS and fluorescence spectroscopy were used for CDs characterization. Results showed that molecular structure of hydrogen bond donor (HBD) had important influence on the CDs properties and doping. The hydrogen bond strength and hydrogen donor ability of DES reduce by the increase of carbon chain length of HBD. DESs with short carbon chain and high content of –NH2 and –OH produce CDs with higher QYs. Some of these CDs were also used as fluorescent nano-probes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Deep eutectic solvent
Choline chloride
Microwave
Solvent effect

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots synthesis in deep eutectic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Absorption and B) fluorescence spectra of CDsDESs.
Chemical compositions of choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents.
Average size, excitation and emission wavelengths, QYs and EDXS analysis of resulting CDs.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for ion and drug detection.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of carbon dots in ten choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents: Effect of solvent molecular structure on carbon dots fluorescence and sensing properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Ilam University, Ilam, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have showed great interest in carbon dots synthesis. Surface and optical characteristics of carbon dots (CDs) can be influenced by solvent during the synthesis. In this study, microwave method was used for CDs synthesis in ten choline chloride based DESs. Glucose was used as a cheap carbon source. Surface functional groups, optical and fluorescence properties of ten CDs were compared. Quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs in choline chloride/urea, choline chloride/glycerol and choline chloride/thiourea DESs were 41.3, 38.7 and 35.5%, respectively. Doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms in the CDsDES1, CDsDES2 and CDsDES4 enhanced the photoluminescence performance. TEM, FT-IR, EDXS and fluorescence spectroscopy were used for CDs characterization. Results showed that molecular structure of hydrogen bond donor (HBD) had important influence on the CDs properties and doping. The hydrogen bond strength and hydrogen donor ability of DES reduce by the increase of carbon chain length of HBD. DESs with short carbon chain and high content of –NH2 and –OH produce CDs with higher QYs. Some of these CDs were also used as fluorescent nano-probes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of the nanocarbon family with sizes below 10 nm, abundant functional groups (e.g. carboxyl hydroxyl, carbonyl) and emission in the Vis-NIR range [1]. Carbon nanodots have attracted interests such as, high chemical stability, high water solubility, rich surface chemistry, easy-preparation at large-scale with low cost, strong and tunable fluorescence emission and excellent biocompatibility. They were used as greener probes in bioimaging and biosensing [2], and drug/gene delivery [3].

Carbon dots can be prepared by a various methods, such as electrochemical [4], pyrolysis and solvothermal [5], laser ablation [6], hydrothermal [7], ultrasonic [8] and microwave carbonization [9,10]. Advantages of microwave method are easy and fast preparation, efficient energy consumption and low cost due to uniform and rapid heating. Microwave cannot directly cleave molecular bonds but in microwave assisted methods, molecular dipolar polarization or ionic induction produce higher energy by direct interaction with solvent and reagent molecules [11].

Most of synthesis methods are in solvents. Solvent properties affect the product morphology and performance. Between environmental friendly solvents, ionic liquids (ILs) and deep eutectic solvents (DESs) have a lot of attention. In 1914, ionic liquids were reported by Walden [12]. An ionic liquid has an organic cation and a bulky inorganic anion. During these years, they were considered as green solvents, but nowadays, it is well known that many of them are volatile, flammable, and toxic. Deep eutectic solvents as highly structured supramolecular solvents were first introduced by Abbott et al. [13]. They are formed by the hydrogen bonding between hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and hydrogen bond donor (HBD) at a certain molar ratio and temperature. Both of ILs and DESs are greener than conventional solvents. Similarities of ILs and DESs are wide liquid ranges, low volatilities, highly tunable and ability to dissolve wide range of compounds [14]. Both of them have high viscosity and density that restrict their practical use. Their viscosity and density change with their composition and temperature. DESs properties can be modified by the proper selection of starting compounds, molar ratio, and addition of water as an additional component. High water content can decompose the DES. Controlled addition of co-solvents like water (a cheap and economical solvent) is advantageous [15] because alter the DESs properties without disrupting its structure. Phase behavior and separation mechanism of the both of these solvents were simulated by molecular dynamics [16], theoretical models [17,18], and COSMO-SAC [19].

Reagents, solvent, passivation agent and synthesis method affect the fluorescent properties of the CDs [20]. Reaction solvent has a significant role on CDs properties. CDs were synthesized in different media such as water [21], diethylene glycol [22], ionic liquids (ILs) [23–25] and deep eutectic solvent (DES) [26]. Low volatility, substrate–reactant solubility, and high thermal stabilities are advantages of carbonization in ILs and DESs with respect to conventional organic solvents. They also can provide more doping elements for CDs. It was proved that ILs are good medium for absorbing MWs [25]. Nevertheless, ILs are expensive that restricted its pilot application. Recently, deep eutectic solvents as supramolecular solvents are used for the CDs synthesis and literature search showed that few studies are available for the synthesis of CDs in DESs [26–31]. Among different types of HBA, choline chloride is biodegradable and non-toxic that are similar to B vitamins. In choline chloride based DESs, physicochemical properties of solvent was changed with HBD structure. Amine groups (i.e. urea and thiourea) have the greatest ability for hydrogen bond formation when mixed with choline chloride [32].

DESs such as ILs can be used as both solvent and doping agent. This leads to reduction in time and chemicals consumption [26]. It was proven that heteroatom doping changes fluorescence quantum yield, the value of d002 (spacing between graphene layers in CDs), crystal structure, electronic structure, energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO and surface functional groups in CDs. Non-metal dopants such as N, S, P, B, F, Cl, Si, I, Se and Te and metal dopants such as Cu, Gd, La, Mn, Zn, Ge, Co, Fe and Ru were used in doped CDs [33–35]. Among the various non-metals dopants, N is a very efficient.

CDs are usually synthesized in solvents and also used in these media, so it is important to study the structure–property relationship between the solvents and CDs morphology and performance. In this work, solvent-engineered CDs synthesis was designed to study the influence of DES structure on CDs properties during the synthesis. Ten choline chloride-based deep eutectic solvents were prepared and used as reaction media, doping, passivation and modification agent. Carbon source was glucose. In all of these DESs, choline chloride was used as hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA). Therefore, change in the structure of hydrogen bond donor (HBD) affect the optical and luminescence properties of synthesized CDs. Surface functional groups, optical and fluorescence properties of ten CDs were compared. Effects of molecular structure of HBD and carbon chain length were also studied. Some of these CDs were also used as fluorescent nano-probes.

Thiourea (CH4N2S), urea (CH4N2O), acetamide (C2H5NO), glycerol (C3H8O3), choline chloride (C5H14ClNO), gallic acid (C7H6O5), glucose (C6H12O6), citric acid (C6H8O7), caffeic acid (C9H8O4), adipic acid (C6H10O4), oxalic acid (C2H2O4), and benzoic acid (C7H6O2) were obtained from Merck. Deionized water is used throughout the experiments.

Size, FT-IR, UV–Vis, fluorescence, and elemental study were done by TEM (MC30 Philips), Vertex 70 FT-IR, Varian Cary 300 Bio, Cary eclipse and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (MIRA II TESCAN), respectively.

In typical preparation, choline chloride (as HBA molecule in all of the studied DESs in this work) was mixed with HBD molecules at certain molar ratios. These HBD molecules and molar ratios are listed in Table 1 . Mixture (in a 10 mL beaker) was heated overnight at 80–100 °C with gentle stirring until a homogeneous and transparent liquid was appeared. Fresh DESs were used for the synthesis of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots synthesis in deep eutectic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis, 0.1 g of glucose was dissolved in 7 mL of DES until no solid is observed. Then, the solution was heated in a microwave oven at 450 W for 5 min. After the breaker cooled down to room temperature, the resulting brown-colored solid was dissolved in 10 mL water, filtered by 0.2 μm filter, and centrifuged (15 min, 12000 rpm). The synthesized CDs had hydrophilic nature. In many biological and environmental measurements, water is selected as medium. Therefore, we finally dispersed the synthesized CDs in water. CDs fluorescence was measured in a 1-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature (λex and λem for each CDs were listed in Table 2 ). Slit widths for the excitation and emission were 5 and 10 nm, respectively.

Quantum yield of CDs was determined by integrating of spectral fluorescent photon flux and measuring absorption fraction at λex = 320 nm for reference (QYquinine sulfate = 0.54) and carbon dots [38]. Quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4, QYR = 0.54) was used as reference. The reference and CDs absorbance were measured at λex = 320 nm and their solutions were diluted until absorbances were less than 0.1 at λex. The fluorescence spectra (370–600 nm) of the reference and CDs were also recorded when λex = 320 nm. The quantum yield was calculated as:
QY

A R

)

)

η

2
where subscripts X and R denote sample and reference, η is the refractive index of solvent. The second term is spectrally integrated photon flux at the detector (the area under the corrected emission spectrum I c(λ ex, λ em)). A is absorbance (the third term in above equation is the fraction of the excitation light absorbed).

TEM images of the synthesized CDs in the different DESs are shown in Figs. 1 and 2
. The CDs were regular in shapes. As shown in Fig. 1, the CDsDES1-CDsDES4 are well-dispersed with very small size in the range from 2 to 9 nm. The DESs used in the synthesis can regulate the lateral sizes of the CDs. In many articles, the effect of solvent has been investigated after the synthesis of carbon dots [36–38]. In these works, CDs was first synthesized and then dispersed in other solvents based on the required application. However, in this study, different solvents (10 DESs) were used in synthesis step. In all of these ten syntheses, choline chloride was used as HBA and glucose as a carbon source. The only difference between them was the structure of the HBD molecule that used in the preparation of the DESs. All of synthesized CDs were finally dispersed in water for further characterization studies. The results indicate that different HBDs can tune the nucleation and growth size. Effect of the HBD structure on the CD size is:

Urea (2 NH2, 2 nm) < acetamide (1 NH2, 2.5 nm) < glycerol (3 OH, 4 nm) < thiourea (2 NH2 + S atom, 9 nm) < citric acid (1 OH + 3 COOH, 26 nm) < adipic acid (2 COOH, 35 nm) < oxalic acid (2 COOH, 47 nm) < benzoic acid (1 COOH + aromatic ring, 50 nm) < gallic acid (3 OH + 1 COOH + aromatic ring, 70 nm) < caffeic acid (2 OH + 1 COOH + aromatic ring, 120 nm).

In parentheses, functional groups of each HBD molecule and average size of the synthesized CDs were shown. As shown, HBDs with amine functional group produced the smallest carbon dots. Acidic HBDs are at the bottom of the list and produce larger carbon dots. The largest carbon dots are produced by aromatic HBDs such as benzoic, gallic and caffeic acids. The presence of sulfur atom (urea vs. thiourea) caused an increase in the CDs size from 2 to 9 nm. Solvent H-bonding strength, surface functional groups and charges can affect the carbonization, nucleation and growth of the CDs.

FT–IR study was used to characterize the CDsDES functional groups. FT-IR spectra (Fig. 3 ) of the most CDs showed similar stretching vibrations characteristics such as alcoholic and carboxylic acid –OH (3200–3650 cm−1), amine and amide –NH (3300–3500 cm−1), C–H stretching vibrations (2850–2970 and 1340–1470 cm−1), carboxylic and amide carbonyl C = O (1690–1760 cm−1), alcohol, ether and carboxylic acid C-O (1050–1300 cm−1) and C-Cl stretching vibrations (600–800 cm−1) [26,39]. The FT-IR spectra of the CDsDES2 showed sulfoxide S = O stretching vibration (1030–1070 cm−1) that was attributed to the doping of S atoms by DES2 (choline chloride + thiourea). Doping of Cl, N and O atoms were also observed as mentioned surface functional groups. The FT-IR spectra of CDsDES5 and CDsDES9 had the weakest peak intensities that indicate low functionality in the surface of these CDs. They had the highest %C in EDXS analysis (Table 2, shown as bold numbers) and therefore, the lowest total content of N, O and Cl atoms.

Table 2 summarizes the EDXS analysis of synthesized CDs (percentage of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, and chloride based on Ka peaks). As shown, the successful incorporation of Cl and N atoms into all CDs confirm from the EDXS spectra. CDsDES2 doped with Cl, N and S atoms. The doping contents in synthesized CDs were 6.6–18.8, 4.5–25.7, 13.1–45.5 and 3.8% for N, O, Cl, and S, respectively. The results in the Table 2 were analyzed to find the relationship between the percentage of atoms and CDs quantum yield and size. QY improvement after nitrogen atom doping was previously reported [40]. Nitrogen has same size as carbon atom and it can change surface structure (HOMO-LUMO energy gap) and increase radiative recombinations [41]. Amounts and types of heteroatoms can increase the fluorescence efficiency. Linear regression analysis were performed and the highest correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.77) was found between N% and quantum yield. There were no correlations between the O%, Cl% or sum of the heteroatom percent and QY or CDs size. An overview in Table 2 also shows that CDs with the highest N% had the highest quantum yields and the smallest sizes. The largest CDs (CD7, 120 nm) had the lowest total heteroatom percent (39.1%).

The synthesized CDs in all DES types showed two absorption bands (Fig. 4 A). Absorption difference may be due to the different carbon cores and surface defects. Bands at 220 nm were attributed to the π → π* transition in graphitic core of CDs [26]. Absorption band at 380 nm for CDsDES1 and CDsDES2 was assigned for the n → n* transitions of the C = O band. Surface doping leads to the n → π* transition [42]. CDsDES4 and CDsDES8 had strong absorbance below 270 nm for π → π* transition due to the aromatic sp2 C = C bond [26]. CDsDES5, CDsDES6, CDsDES7 and CDsDES10 had weak peaks at 290 nm for π → π* of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons [43].

Excitation and emission wavelengths of the synthesized CDs are shown in Table 2. The maximum emission was located at 471 nm when excitation was 390 nm. Excitation wavelengths were 320–390 nm and emission spectra are shown in Fig. 4B. Surface defect, band-gap transitions, surface structure and size can cause these differences [44]. Comparison of their fluorescence intensities confirmed that CDs synthesized in DES1 had higher intensity than that of other prepared CDsDES. As shown in Table 2, the quantum yield of CDs varied from 13.17 to 41.30%, and CDsDES1 had the highest QY (41.30 %). Reason of the high QY of CDsDES1 might be from surface amine groups that passives CDsDES1 surface state [45]. CDsDES8 (choline chloride/adipic acid) expressed lower fluorescence. DES/glucose interaction depends on hydrogen bond strength of HBA/HBD [46]. Adipic acid has weak hydrogen bonds with choline chloride. Therefore, surface of these CDs had apolar features and expressed lower fluorescence in water (Fig. 4B) [47]. Structural features of reactants such as carbon chain length and functional groups had important influence on the fluorescent properties. The hydrogen bond strength and hydrogen donor ability of DES reduce by the increase of carbon chain length of HBD and electron donating of the alkyl group [48]. In comparison of CD9chloride/oxalic acid (QY = 15.86%) and CD8choline chloride/adipic acid (QY = 13.17%), increase of carbon chain (C4 for adipic acid vs. C2 for oxalic acid) led to the decrease of quantum yield. In comparison of CD4choline chloride/glycerol (QY = 38.75%) and CD6choline chloride/citric acid (QY = 32.8%) with same carbon chain length (C3), change of functional groups from –OH to –COOH led to the decease of QY. Increase of the QY with increase of hydroxyl groups was observed for CD5choline chloride/gallic acid (QY = 19.56%) vs. CD10chloride/benzoic acid (QY = 17.33%) that have similar structure but gallic acid has three –OH groups (Table 1). In comparison of CD1choline chloride/urea (QY = 41.30%) and CD2choline chloride/thiourea (QY = 35.50%) with similar structure, change of heteroatom from oxygen to sulfur decreases the QY. In comparison of CD1choline chloride/urea (QY = 41.30%) and CD3choline chloride/acetamide (QY = 24.50%), change of –NH2 by –CH3 group decreases the QY, intensely. Therefore, DESs with short carbon chain and high content of –NH2 and –OH produce CDs with higher quantum yields.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the CDs had great photo-bleaching resistance and the fluorescence intensities were stable for more than two months and retained about 60% under room temperature. This optical stability is amenable for bio-sensing applications. Fluorescence intensity CDsDES5, CDsDES7 and CDsDES10 were less than 40%.

Two parts in CDs are responsible for fluorescent properties: carbon-core and surface with functional groups. A common functionalization strategy is heteroatom doping that provides new performance for different applications. Functional groups containing N, O, S atoms on CDs surface can coordinate or reduce different metallic ions or organic molecules. These reactions change the fluorescence of the CDs. Therefore, this property can be used as an efficient strategy in fluorescent nanoprobes design. Knowledge about the effect of solvent during synthesis, doping, type and amount of functional groups help to design the engineered-CDs with better selectivity and sensivity. As shown in Table 3 , CDs prepared in DES1, DES2, DES3 and DES4 (the highest QYs) were used for the determination of Fe3+, Co2+ and drugs such as atorvastatin, naproxen, cetirizine and enrofloxacin.

It may be interesting to compare surface and spectral properties of CDs synthesized in DESs with other solvents. But this key point should be noted that carbon dots synthesis is a multivariate experiment and different variables affect the final CDs properties. These variables are initial reactants (biomass or pure chemicals), their molar ratio, reaction solvent (type, pH [50], polarity and hydrogen bond), doping element, type of dopant [51], molar ratio of dopant [50], synthesis method (hydrothermal, microwave, ultrasonic, etc.), experimental parameters (temperature, time, and radiation power), purification steps, and final dispersion solvent. Quality, accuracy and precision of characterization instruments are also affect results. Therefore, comparison of synthesized CDs is meaningful when results are obtained under same and controlled experimental conditions.

In this study, we used choline chloride (constant HBA) and different HBDs for solvation and source of doping atoms such as N, S, Cl. Glucose was used as carbon source (constant value). Synthesis method was microwave. Finally synthesized N, Cl-doped CDs (Table 2) were dispersed in water and their properties such as quantum yield, size, excitation and emission wavelengths, and dopant atoms percent were measured. As mentioned, all experimental variables were constant except HBD structure. Therefore, a solvent-engineered CDs synthesis was designed to study the influence of DES structure on CDs properties during the synthesis.

There is a few works about effect of reaction-solvent and dispersion-solvent on CDs properties that were recently reviewed [35]. In the most of previously reported works, citric acid or biomass was used for synthesis. Synthesis method and conditions were also very different. Glucose-based synthesis of CDs was reviewed [52]. Based on the our best search, there are no reports about synthesis of CDs from glucose and one of the acetamide, glycerol, gallic acid, caffeic acid, adipic acid, citric acid, oxalic acid and benzoic acid. These carbon dots were synthesized for the first time. Only a few reports are available about synthesis of CDs from glucose and urea (Table 4 ). As mentioned in ref. 35, CDs were synthesized by citric acid/urea in different reaction-solvents such as water, N, N-dimethylformamide, ethanol and formic acid. There is no systematic study about CDs synthesis by glucose/urea in different solvents that need to further investigation. As shown in Table 4, water is reaction and dispersion solvent. CDs were synthesized by glucose/urea had smaller size (3–5.6 nm) with respect to that from glucose (8.9 nm). CDs synthesized by microwave method had smaller size (3, 3.6 nm) with respect to hydrothermal method (5, 5.6 nm). CDs synthesized by glucose/urea-choline chloride (DES) had the smallest size and highest quantum yield. As mentioned at the beginning of this section, comparison of synthesized CDs is meaningful when results are obtained under same and controlled experimental conditions by a research team.

In this work, solvent-controlled CDs syntheses were conducted and the effects of different DESs on CDs morphology and performance during the synthesis were studied and a structure–property relationship were established between solvents and CDs properties such as size and quantum yield. Carbon dots were prepared in ten choline chloride based DESs by microwave irradiation. Doping contents of heteroatoms can be easily adjusted by simply changing the solvent. DES can be simultaneously used as a passivation and doping agent and solvent. TEM, UV–Vis, FT-IR, EDXS and fluorescence spectroscopy were used for CDs characterization. Quantum yield of synthesized CDs varied from 13.17 to 41.30%, and the highest QY = 41.30% was for CDs synthesized in the choline chloride/urea deep eutectic solvent. Results showed that molecular structure of hydrogen bond donor, its carbon chain length and content of hydroxyl and amine groups of HBD had important influence on the CDs characteristics. The prepared CDs in deep eutectic show intense fluorescent emission, good photo-stability and quantum yields. They can also be used for fluorescence nano-sensing.
Reza Tabaraki: Supervision. Fereshteh Nazari: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
